structure.title: Introduction and functional overview
structure.summary: Transformers in AUTOSAR systems enable data transformation mechanisms to linearize and transform data. These transformers can be concatenated into chains, which are executed by the RTE for both intra-ECU and inter-ECU communication. The RTE handles the execution of these chains for communication configured to require data transformation. The mechanism supports linearization and transformation of data across different communication contexts. Transformer chains are integral to enabling transformed data exchange in AUTOSAR systems.

structure.title: A transformer provides well defined function signatures per each communication relation (port based and signal based), which is marked for transformation. The function signature depends on the transmitted data elements (Client/Server operation signature or Sender/Receiver interface signature) only. The output of a transformer will be always a linear byte array.

A more powerful system can chain multiple transformers where the input of the first transformer in the chain gets the data from the RTE. Each following transformer uses the output of the preceding transformer as input. All transformers following the first one then have generic signature with just a byte array as IN and OUT parameter. Such an architecture could be used to design systems, where you can flexibly add functionality like safety or security protection to a serialized stream.

structure.title: Dependencies to other modules
structure.summary: The module does not have dependencies on any AUTOSAR SWS modules. No external AUTOSAR SWS components are required for its functionality. The absence of dependencies ensures standalone operability. All required functionalities are self-contained within the module. No inter-module communication or integration with AUTOSAR SWS is necessary.

nodes.title: file structure
nodes.summary: The code file structure for transformers is defined by [2, SWS BSW General], as all transformers are implemented as BSW modules. This standardization ensures consistency across transformer implementations. Deviations from the general structure are explicitly documented in the specific SWS documents for individual transformers. The reference [2, SWS BSW General] serves as the foundational framework for organizing transformer-related code. Specific transformer variations are addressed through their respective SWS documentation, maintaining clarity and modularity.

nodes.title: Header file structure
nodes.summary: The header file structure for transformers is defined by the [2, SWS BSW General] document, as all transformers are classified as BSW modules. This standard applies uniformly to all transformers unless specified otherwise. Deviations from the general structure are explicitly detailed in the SWS documents specific to individual transformers. The reference [2] provides the foundational framework, while specific SWS documents address unique requirements.

structure.title: Requirements Tracing
structure.summary: The "Requirements Tracing" section maps SRS (Software Requirements Specification) requirements to their corresponding SWS (Software Specification) requirements. Two tables detail this linkage, with each row

structure.title: A transformers takes data from the RTE, works on them and returns the output back to the RTE. It can both serialize/linearize data (transform them from a structured into a linear form) and transform (modify or extend linear data) them (e.g add a checksum).
A transformer is no library because transformers can hold an internal state but they can work as well stateless.
structure.summary: A transformer is not a library because stateful transformers

structure.title: A further example of data transformation is shown in Figure 7.2. Here the use-case of intra-ECU data transformation is addressed.
structure.summary: Figure 7.2 illustrates intra-ECU data transformation, emphasizing the need for a transformer to address architectural differences between sender and target ECUs (e.g., bit-width, endianness). The transformer must explicitly define endianness, data element ordering in complex data, data semantics (e.g., two’s complement, character encoding), source/target data types via PortPrototype/SystemSignal, and padding. These requirements are static and can be hardcoded into the transformer during RTE generation to ensure consistent on-wire formatting. All specifications are derived from upstream requirements SRS_Xfrm_00008.

structure.title: A transformer gets its input data via a pointer which destination can vary in length. Therefore, an implementation of a transformer has to cope with input data which are longer than expected.
structure.summary: A transformer must handle input data via a pointer with variable lengths, tolerating unexpected data while discarding excess, as specified by SRS_Xfrm_00005. Configurability of PortInterfaceMapping

structure.title: A transformer will usually work on the data and/or generate some protocol information which are stored in a header and/or footer of the output. Therefore it needs a place to write the result to. Transformers can work with two buffer handling modes: In-place buffer and out-of-place buffer. Which one is used is determined by the configuration in the [3, System Template] and influences the transformer’s interface.
structure.summary: Transformers operate using in-place or out-of-place buffering, determined by the [3, System Template] configuration, which affects their interface. In-place buffering reuses the input

nodes.title: Transformer Classes
nodes.summary: Transformers are categorized into classes due to their diverse functionalities. Different types of transformers serve entirely distinct purposes, necessitating classification. The categorization into classes reflects the varying roles and operational characteristics of these transformers. This classification system ensures clarity in identifying and applying the appropriate transformer for specific tasks. The text emphasizes that

structure.title: A transformer class shall contain all transformers which provide similar functionality. At most one transformer of each transformer class shall be allowed per transformer chain.
structure.summary: A transformer class groups transformers with similar functionality, and each chain may include at most one transformer from each class. The current transformer classes defined are Serializer, Safety, Security, and Custom. Additional classes may be introduced in future AUTOSAR releases. The rule ensures consistency and avoids redundancy in transformer chains by restricting class-specific instances.

structure.title: A serializer transformer accepts complex data (either a Sender/Receiver data element or a Client/Server operation with its arguments) or no data (Trigger communication) from the RTE and provides the resulting byte array as an <u>ISignal</u> or part of <u>IPdu</u>, which is finally transmitted to the receiver by the COM stack.
structure.summary: The serializer transformer processes complex data (Sender/Receiver elements or Client/Server operations with arguments) or no data (Trigger communication) from the RTE, converting it into a byte array as an ISignal or part of an IPdu for transmission via the COM stack. It must serialize all possible complex data inputs using a defined algorithm, as per SRS_Xfrm_00001, and exclusively support "new-world" variable-size array data types, excluding "old-world" types. Deserializer transformers on the receiver side must return all or a subset of

structure.title: A safety transformer protects the communication against unintentional modifications to ensure a safe data transmission.
structure.summary: The safety transformer is mandated to protect inter-ECU communication for safety-related SWCs, ensuring both the correct order and content of data transmissions. It achieves this by implementing mechanisms such as sequence counters and checksums, which fulfill safety requirements. The upstream requirement SRS_Xfrm_00001 specifies these protective measures to prevent unintentional modifications during data transfer. The transformer’s design guarantees reliable and secure data transmission by validating transmission sequence and integrity. These safeguards are critical for maintaining safe and accurate communication in safety-critical systems.

structure.title: A security transformer protects the communication against intentional modifications to ensure security of the bus communication.
structure.summary: The security transformer is required to protect inter-ECU communication for security-related SWCs, ensuring data authenticity, integrity, and freshness. These requirements are fulfilled through mechanisms like sequence counters and checksums, which address intentional modifications to bus communication. The upstream requirements (SRS_Xfrm_00001) mandate the transformer's role in safeguarding data transmissions against tampering. Specific technical measures, such as sequence counters and checksums, are explicitly cited as examples to meet these security objectives. The transformer's implementation directly aligns with preserving the security and reliability of communication across ECUs.

nodes.title: Custom
nodes.summary: Custom transformers are not standardized by AUTOSAR but can be defined by any development party to address non-standardized transformation needs. They can be implemented as CDDs (Consistency Diagnostic Definitions) within the development workflow.

nodes.title: Error Handling
nodes.summary: Transformers communicate errors to the RTE via return codes, which determines whether the transformer chain continues or aborts. Errors are categorized as soft (0x01–0x7F) or hard (0x80–0xFF): soft errors allow the chain to proceed, while hard errors trigger an abort due to irreparable data issues. Hard errors require the output buffer to remain unchanged, and transformers must only return errors defined within their class’s predefined set. Upstream requirements mandate that hard errors indicate invalid output, while soft errors signal non-critical warnings.

structure.title: AUTOSAR CP R25-11
structure.summary: The error handling in AUTOSAR CP R25-11 specifies that soft errors in early transformer stages of a chain may be masked by subsequent hard errors in later transformers. For example, an E2E transformer detecting a corrupted or masqueraded message triggers a soft error

nodes.title: Errors of Safety Transformers
nodes.summary: The "Errors of Safety Transformers" section outlines error codes for safety transformer communication, categorized under "Soft" error types. Errors like E_SAFETY_VALID_REP, E_SAFETY_VALID_SEQ, and E_SAFETY_VALID_ERR indicate valid data despite counter issues (repeated counter, counter jump, or check

nodes.title: Errors of Security Transformers
nodes.summary: The section outlines errors specific to security transformers, noting that values 0x04, 0x24, 0x34, and 0x44 are reserved by the E2E Library. It categorizes errors into **Soft** and **Hard** types, with descriptions detailing issues like invalid communication, state machine deactivation, counter anomalies, and failed integrity checks. Errors such as `E_SAFETY_INVALID_NND` (0x45) and `E_SAFETY_NOSM_ERR` (0x63) indicate safety violations or data corruption, while `E_SAFETY_HARD_RUNTIMEERROR` (0xFF) signifies critical failures preventing output generation. Additional errors like `E_SEC_NOT_AUTH` (0x01) and `E_SEC_NOT

nodes.title: Production Errors
nodes.summary: The "Production Errors" section states there are no production errors. Transformer-specific errors are categorized into soft (0x01–0x7F) and hard (0x80–0xFF) types, with E_OK (0x00) indicating no error. Additional error codes include <MIP>_E_UNINIT (0x01) for premature API calls, <MIP>_E_INIT_FAILED (0x02) for invalid configuration sets, <MIP>_E_PARAM

nodes.title: XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE
nodes.summary: The section defines the Extended Production Error **XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE**, which must exist for every transformer where the parameter _XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE is set. This requirement is tied to the upstream specifications **SRS_BSW_00466** and **SRS_BSW_00469**, both of which are explicitly referenced. The error is mandated to ensure proper handling of malformed messages in transformer implementations. The duplication of upstream requirements in the text may indicate a formatting or source control artifact but does not alter the technical obligation. The summary preserves all stated conditions and references without extrapolation.

nodes.title: Error Notification
nodes.summary: The error **XFRM_E_MALFORMED_MESSAGE** occurs when a transformer fails to produce output due to malformed input data, as defined in [2, SWS BSW General]. The long description clarifies that the input data does not conform to the transformer's specification, preventing valid output generation. Detection criteria distinguish between a **Fail** (input format violation) and **Pass** (input format compliance). Monitoring occurs on every transformer execution, with no secondary parameters or time requirements specified.

nodes.title: Imported types
nodes.summary: The "Imported types" section specifies that transformers must use the `ImplementationDataTypes` defined by RTE in the Module Interlink Types Header file, as mandated by upstream requirement SRS_Xfrm_00002. This header file is referenced in SWS_Rte_07503, which outlines the structure for module interlink types. The requirement ensures consistency by enforcing the use of RTE-defined data types within the transformer’s implementation. No additional data types or external definitions are permitted beyond those specified in the referenced standards. The focus is on adherence to predefined types for interoperability and compliance.

nodes.title: **\<Mip\>\_ExtractProtocolHeaderFields**
nodes.summary: The function **\<Mip\>\_ExtractProtocolHeaderFields** (Service ID 0x05, synchronous, reentrant) extracts protocol header fields related to message type and result from a buffer. It takes a buffer pointer and length as input, and outputs message type and result via pointers. On successful extraction, it returns **E_OK**,

nodes.title: \<Mip\>\_<transformerId\>
nodes.summary: The function `<Mip>_<transformerId>` is a synchronous, reentrant service in Sender/Receiver communication, used to transform data elements. It takes an input `dataElement`, an output buffer (`buffer`), and a buffer length pointer (`bufferLength`), returning a `uint8` status (0x00 for success, 0x01–0xff for errors). The function is defined in `<Mip>.h` with service ID 0x03 and is the first transformer in a chain

structure.title: Configuration specification
structure.summary: This section outlines configuration parameters and their organization into containers, referencing Chapter 10.1 for foundational concepts and a parameter specification template. Section 10.2 defines the structure and parameters of transformers, which are configured at the system level using [3, System Template] and at the software component level via [5, Software Component Template]. These configurations enable the generation of a basic **EcuC** for the transformer. The chapter retains Chapter 10.1 to ensure clarity and comprehensive understanding of the specification.

nodes.title: Containers and configuration parameters
nodes.summary: The section outlines configuration parameters for a general transformer setup, referencing Chapter 7 (Functional Specification) and Chapter 8 (API specification) for detailed parameter definitions. Specific transformers utilize the **EcuC** container, which is populated with their specific contents. The **EcuC** is generated automatically from the **DataTransformationSet** data, as the transformer generator possesses all required information to populate it. The **[ECUC_Xfrm_00014]** section defines the **EcucModuleDef Xfrm**, which is a critical component of the configuration structure. This ensures modular and standardized configuration management for transformers.

nodes.title: XfrmGeneral
nodes.summary: The **XfrmGeneral** container, part of the Xfrm module, contains general configuration parameters for the module. It is a single-instance container (multiplicity 1) under the Xfrm parent, with no post-build variant support and pre-compile variant configuration. The container includes parameters such as **XfrmDevErrorDetect**, a boolean parameter that enables or disables development error detection and notification (default: false), and other parameters like **XfrmInstanceld** and **XfrmVersionInfoApi**. The **XfrmImplementationMapping** container, also included, manages mappings of transformers to BswModuleEntries in a

structure.title: Referenced Meta Classes
structure.summary: The "Referenced Meta Classes" section outlines two key meta-classes: **ArrayImplPolicyEnum** and **BswModuleEntry**. **ArrayImplPolicyEnum** defines configuration options for array payload implementation, with literals `payloadAsArray` and `payloadAsPointerTo Array`, each tagged with specific indices. **BswModuleEntry** represents a C-function prototype for a BSW module, with attributes like `callType`, `executionContext`, and `isReentrant` governing service behavior, and it is exclusive to the AUTOSAR Classic Platform. The section also details aggregations and base classes for these meta-classes, along with XML and tagging annotations for attribute management.

nodes.title: 4.2. A.27: Transformation Technology
nodes.summary: The **TransformationTechnology** class is a transformer within a transformer chain, inheriting from _ARObject, Identifiable, MultiLanguageReferrable, and Referrable. It is aggregated by **DataTransformationSet.transformationTechnology** and includes attributes such as **bufferProperties** (aggregated), **hasInternalState**, **needsOriginalData**, **protocol**, **transformationDescription** (aggregated with stereotypes *atpSplittable*, *atpVariation* and tag *atp.Splitkey*), **transformerClass** (enum), and **version** (with tag *vh.latestBindingTime=postBuild*).
